学 HIRANUMA
HIGH SCHOOL
YOKOHAMA

Exchange Student Diaries · Vol. 01

A Full Day in a
Japanese High School

from 7am alarms to matcha ice cream disasters — here's how it actually goes ✦

First things first — where even is this school?

Hiranuma Koko sits tucked into Yokohama's bustle — a fifteen-minute walk from the station, which means a fifteen-minute walk every single morning. Uphill. Past convenience stores and vending machines that test your willpower daily. It's not the most dramatic commute in the world, but when you're in Japan, even a walk to school feels like the opening sequence of an anime.

The building itself is tall and grey and real — not the dreamy pastel school from every manga ever. But the moment you step inside, something shifts. There's a rhythm here. A structure. And once you get used to it, it's genuinely kind of beautiful.

平沼高校

Hiranuma High School, Yokohama

TYPE · Public / Government School
LOCATION · Yokohama, Kanagawa
WALK · 15 min from the eki
FLOORS · 5 (ichi-nen-sei @ top 😤)
VIBE · Structured chaos + warmth
arriving before the golden hour hits different
Morning Ritual

7:00 AM — Rise & Shokupan

The alarm goes off. You lie there for exactly as long as you can afford to, then get up, get dressed, and head downstairs where your host mom has already made breakfast. One slice of shokupan — that pillowy soft Japanese milk bread — with half a fried egg, crisp lettuce, and a generous squeeze of Kewpie mayo. It sounds simple. It is simple. It is also the best thing you've ever eaten at 7am.

arigato hosuto okaa-san 🙏 kewpie mayo daisuki!!!
07:00
08:00
The Commute

8:00 AM — The Uphill Walk

Walk uphill for five minutes. Catch the bus. Reach the station. Walk to school from Yokohama station. The routine sounds tedious but it never really feels that way — there's something about navigating a Japanese city in the morning, watching everyone moving with quiet purpose, that makes you feel like you're part of something.

me and my friend every morning like two little penguins in our uniforms
Arrival

8:30 AM — Five Floors & Greetings

Here's the thing about Hiranuma: the first-years (ichi-nen-sei) are on the FIFTH floor. The third-years — who need to focus and study and get into universities — get the third floor, closest to everything. The first-years get cardio. Every. Single. Day. I climbed those stairs every morning, dropped my stuff at my locker outside the classroom, and greeted my friends with the particular kind of warmth that only people who also just climbed five floors can share.

WHY DID ICHI NEN SEI GET THE TOP FLOOR HUH??? still bitter tbh
08:30
"The sensei stood up, bowed to the class — and then looked at me and said 'Namaste.'" — the moment I knew this school was going to be okay

日記 — DAILY LOG — HIRANUMA KOKO

Classes, characters & chaos

My homeroom teacher — absolute legend — used to live in India in the eighties as a volunteer. He greeted me in Namaste every single morning like it was the most natural thing in the world. SAIKOU.

History class had the funniest teacher I've ever encountered in an academic setting. Maths A was taught by a sir who — and I say this with full admiration — bagged his university senpai and went on all sorts of side quests as a bachelor. Maths B had this incredibly kind older lady who taught trigonometry with the patience of someone who has seen it all and remains undefeated.

Then there was the daily diary. Every day, one student writes the class diary — all the subjects from that day, and people draw the most incredible things in it. When it was my turn, I drew Paimon. And then, on behalf of my friend the class president, I wrote: "Hi I am lonely on Valentine's Day. If anyone goes out with me I'll give them 1 million yen and a bouquet of red roses."

My homeroom teacher read it. He wrote: "Really?"

* sensei-san if you're reading this — it was a joke. probably.
my class 🫶 the best people
shodou elective — i cooked SO hard
fifth floor does have one perk

12:30 PM · Lunch

The Bento & The Incident

Lunch meant my host mom's bento — carefully packed, delicious, a little act of love every day. My classmates ate curry sets from the cafeteria. We sat together and ate and talked in a mix of broken Japanese and enthusiastic gesturing and it was genuinely one of the best parts of every day.

And then I discovered the ice cream vending machine in the basement. Matcha flavour. Obviously. But there wasn't enough time to finish it before the bell, so I made what I still believe was a perfectly reasonable decision: I put it in my bento bag.

Reader, it melted. Matcha everywhere. Bag ruined. I am so sorry, okaa-san.

3:00 PM · Club Activities

Shodou & Kyudo

My elective was calligraphy — 書道, shodou — and I'm not going to be humble about this: I cooked. The ink, the brush, the rhythm of it. Something about sitting at that desk with Japanese characters slowly taking shape under my hand felt deeply right.

Then kyudo club — traditional Japanese archery. My friends were kind enough to teach me, walking me through every tiny detail of the form. The stillness of it. The way you have to be completely present. I was terrible. It was wonderful.

free at 5pm. went home after loitering around for a while, obviously
what the fifth floor actually gets you — this view at golden hour
